text: So, hi. I'm Matt. My main thing I do, I'm on the core Astro team for the Astro Web Framework.
I also work at Cloudflare, but I'm not here to talk about any of that today.
I'm here to talk about Cirrus, which is my PDS.
So, I have built a PDS that I use to help self-host, and I hopefully might convince some of you people here to self-host your PDS as well.
But first, what is a PDS?
PDS is a personal data server.
It is where all of your stuff is on the AppRoto network.
Now, a lot of people here probably know this, but your PDS holds pretty much everything on the AppRoto network for you.
It's all of your posts, of course, but it's also all of your likes and your friends and your whole social graph,
but it's also everything from any of your other third-party apps that you're working with.
All of the different network, all of the things on the AppRoto network live on your PDS.
They also, all of the blobs live on there as well, which are, you know, the files, the images, and the videos, and things like that.
So, the whole point of a PDS is to make it so that you are able to have the network as decentralized, so that you own all of your data.
And what I didn't realize is, as well as being where all your data is, it's also where you talk to.
So, it's where your API calls go when you're using the network.
So, when you've got the, like, Blue Sky app, for example, on your phone or on the web, it's not talking to Blue Sky when it's connected.
If you look at the network panel, and honestly, I mean, the first time I knew this was when I opened up the network panel
and saw that it was hitting my PDF.
You talk directly to your PDS.
So, all of your connections and everything go directly to your PDS.
So, most of the stuff a PDS does is talking to you.
This, obviously, your PDS doesn't have all of the data on it.
Most of the data on the network is somewhere else.
So, for anything that it doesn't have, like all of your timeline and everything else,
it proxies it to the app view, which, you know, in most cases is Blue Sky, which holds everything.
But how does the relay, how does Blue Sky or the rest of the network get your data?
It's done through a relay.
So, there are two relays that Blue Sky has, U.S. East and U.S. West.
They recently changed them around.
And, basically, your PDS, when you publish something, it sends it out to the relay.
So, the way that works is it's a web socket, like a permanent web socket.
The relay connects to your web socket, and it's, whenever you post something, it sends it out into the relay.
And, as you can probably see from this at the moment, it's not actually doing a huge amount of work, the PDS, per person.
It's only doing something when you post on it or when you make a query on it.
Because the app view isn't, so when somebody goes and looks at your timeline, they aren't hitting your PDS.
What they're doing is it's being relayed to the app view from the relays.
And the relays hold every single post on the network.
Every single record, every single event on the network goes through the relays, and they send it off to the app view.
So, all of the work, really, the heavy work, is done by the relays and the app view.
So, it's a PDS, doesn't actually do a huge amount of work, but, and you can, anyone can host them, but that's not what people are doing.
So, you know, 99 point, this, I mean, these are numbers out of last week.
99.4% of all users are using the Blue Sky PDSs.
They're the mushroom ones.
They're named after mushrooms.
There are some, obviously, fast-growing other PDS hosts.
The new kid on the block is Eurosky.
I'm not including the bridgey ones on here, because they don't really count.
But the bridgey one is like 10 times more than that, but I'm not including it, because they're not real, you know, not real accounts.
So, Eurosky is the new kid.
It's grown very fast and has managed to make it all the way up to almost 0.02% of all users.
I mean, I think, and obviously, Black Sky is also very, you know, is a very solid percentage of users in terms of the ones who are using a third-party PDS or an independent PDS.
And then there's another, you know, less than 3,000 PDSs out there, which, you know, collectively make up, you know, 0.03% of users.
And that includes all of mine.
Now, why is that?
Why is it that people aren't hosting these things?
It's because it's really heavy and not designed for hosting an individual thing.
This is the Blue Sky reference PDS is a, like, a Docker image.
There's a hell of a lot of stuff going on in there.
It's, you know, it's well-documented, and you can host it, and it's great for hosting thousands of users, but it is complete overkill for hosting a single account.
So, I wanted to build something that was easier and that was simpler and was a bit more like how you would host a single website.
You know, if you're deploying a little single-user website, you're not going to spin up, most people are not going to spin up a Docker image.
They're not going to hold, you know, ran up a whole thing that's got, you know, all the databases and all that kind of stuff.
Most people will just deploy it to a serverless platform or something and host it for free somewhere.
And I, you know, I thought, let's, you know, why can't we do PDSs that are as simple as that?
So, I'm going to show doing exactly that.

So, the did is your kind of unique identifier that you can take around even if you change your handle.
Did plc is the centralized one where it's sort of like managed for you on the plc directory, which you'll find at plc.directory, which is hosted by Blue Sky.
Did web is a kind of more simple, but it's got some downsides.

I am going to use the same domain for my did, my handle, and my PDS, which means that I can host them all, which makes everything a hell of a lot easier.

It's storing it in a durable object, which is one of these weird things that we have at Cloudflare that I didn't really understand until I started working there a few months ago.
And as soon as I started working there, I was like, I want to use these for everything because they're amazingly cool.
That's my sales pitch for the day.
But one of the things you can do with a durable object is they come with their own little embedded SQLite database, and you can say where you want it to go.
So, most people would say automatic, which will basically put it near to where you are when you first create it.
But we also, if you want to have data localization, you can have a guaranteed localization in the EU.
So, this one is where it's guaranteed and is GDPR compliant and e-privacy and whatever.

Or you can just say if you want it to be located near, you know, if you're creating it at a conference, which is not where you are, you want to say, actually, I want it to be created somewhere else.
These are not ones that are going to give you a guaranteed location, but I'm going to just choose auto.
So, I said that there was downsides to using a did web.
If you're using did PLC, you need a signing key to sign all of your records.
If you're using PLC, then you can rotate your signing key.

If you are self-hosting your did web and you lose your signing key, you are screwed.

Okay, I've got time to talk about how it works.
So, it's inside Cloudflare.
It's, that's mainly just because it's an easy way for me to put everything all in one place.
And the key part of that is the, the fire hose.
And I'll get into why that, why that's, that's special.
But basically, so as I said, the client is, you know, the browser, the web browser or the, or your mobile app.
And that talks to the worker, which is basically, it's running a HONO little web server on there that just does the XRPC API endpoints.
There's like a load of API endpoints that it serves on there.
And it's, you know, doing the sending messages to it.
And the durable object, which I mentioned there, is where the, the kind of the magic happens in there.
So, it's got a SQLite database in there, which stores your whole repo in there.
All of the MST stuff in there is all one big database or little database, really.
And then there's the fire hose.
Now, the fire hose is the thing that makes it really difficult to host a PDS on a serverless platform in most cases.
So, you can't put it on like an AWS Lambda or something like that because it needs a continuously connected web socket.
The actual thing that made me, like, decide to build this was when I heard about, found out that you could do hibernating web sockets on durable objects.
What that means is that you can have a web socket on this, on this PDS that is open all the time and connected to the relay all the time.
But you're not paying unless you are sending an event.
If there is nothing going from it, it just goes to sleep.
It hibernates it.
But as far as the relay is concerned, it's still connected.
It thinks that it's connected, but it's actually just being held by, you know, some part of our Cloudflow infra or something.
But when you then want to add, send an event, when you're posting something, it wakes it up and pings it off onto the relay and you get charged for a few seconds.
Well, you don't get charged because it will be within the free tier unless, I think, even the craziest of posters won't be able to get outside the free tier on this one.
And then it uses R2 for storing all the blobs.
That's all still inside Cloudflare.
And then it proxies it to the app view, which is, you know, wherever it is.
And I didn't show you a few things.
So there's account migration, which is built in.

You'll put in your password and it will migrate everything.

And that works for all of the, you know, all the third-party services and everything.

This is the thing.
This is you can build one for yourself.
And I think there are, I think that there's a, you know, most people do want to have their PDS hosted by Blue Sky.
You know, that's where it makes sense to do it.
But I think that if we want the app proto network to be properly decentralized, we need to have lots of different ways of hosting.
And I think that, you know, it needs to be easy for a service to have their own PDS.
You know, one of the, any of these other services can provide it for their users.
They want, and, but I think there also needs to be an easy way for people to deploy it themselves.
And I think that one of the things that I really love about this group of all of us, you know, weirdos and most affectionately here is that it reminds me a lot of early web stuff.

And one of the whole things about the early web is that you could, you know, deploy your own stuff and do all kinds of, and I think that the fact that all of the reference architecture of the atmosphere is blue sky stuff.
It means that it's, you know, very much angled, not just towards the social use case, but also to the massive scale.
And it's great that people are building all these other different implementations.
But I think that we need more ways for people to just make this as easy as, you know, sticking something on Netlify or Vercel or Cloudflare or whatever, whatever you think of them.
They are a very, very easy way of putting a website up.
And I wanted this to be a way to make it that easy to put a PDF up.
